14.text: Standard math library, used here for the square root in attention scaling. JAX handles everything else.

15.text: Provides utilities for constructing file paths to locate the dataset on disk.

16.text: Python's built-in random module for shuffling the dataset. JAX has its own separate RNG for tensor operations.

18.text: JAX: Google's functional autodiff framework. A completely different philosophy from PyTorch, no classes, no hidden state, pure functions everywhere.

19.text: jnp is JAX's NumPy replacement, same API, but arrays live on GPU/TPU and support automatic differentiation.

20.text: grad transforms a function into its gradient function. jit compiles Python into optimized machine code. These are JAX's core transformations.

29.text: Download the names dataset from the internet if it is not already present locally.

32.text: URL pointing to the raw names dataset hosted in Karpathy's repository.

34.text: Read the text file and split it into individual name strings, one per line.

35.text: Randomize the training order so the model does not memorize sequences of names.

38.text: The set of unique characters in the dataset forms our vocabulary. Each character gets a fixed integer ID for the model to work with.

39.text: The beginning-of-sequence token doubles as the end-of-sequence marker for name boundaries.

40.text: The vocabulary has 27 tokens: 26 lowercase letters plus the BOS/EOS marker.

47.text: Split attention into 4 parallel heads so each can focus on different character relationships.

48.text: A single transformer layer, keeping the model minimal for this small-scale experiment.

49.text: Names up to 16 characters long fit in the context window. Longer names get truncated, but most fit easily.

50.text: Each of the 4 heads gets 4 dimensions of the 16-dimensional embedding, letting heads specialize on different character relationships.

52.text: JAX's PRNG is explicit, you create a key and split it for each random operation. No hidden global state, unlike NumPy/PyTorch.

55.text: Parameter initializer, same N(0, 0.08) distribution, but using JAX's RNG.

56.text: JAX random requires an explicit key for every call. This makes randomness reproducible and parallelizable.

59.text: Pre-split 20 random keys for parameter initialization. JAX forces you to be explicit about where randomness enters the computation.

60.text: Iterator over keys, each parameter gets its own unique key.

62.text: Parameters are a plain Python dict, no nn.Module, no parameter registration. In JAX, parameters are just data you pass to functions.

63.text: Initialize the token embedding table that maps each character to a learned vector.

64.text: Position embeddings give the model a sense of order. Each slot in the sequence has its own learned vector encoding 'where' a token sits.

65.text: The output head weight matrix that maps from embedding space back to vocabulary logits for next-token prediction.

67.text: Per-layer weights, same structure as previous labs, just stored in a dict.

68.text: Weight matrix that projects the normalized input into query vectors for attention.

69.text: Weight matrix that produces key vectors for positions to be matched against queries.

70.text: Weight matrix that produces value vectors carrying content to be mixed by attention.

71.text: Project the concatenated multi-head output back into the embedding space.

72.text: First MLP weight matrix that expands the representation to 4x the embedding dimension.

73.text: Second MLP weight matrix that compresses back to the original embedding dimension.

75.text: jax.tree.leaves extracts all arrays from the nested dict. JAX treats dicts as "pytrees", a core abstraction for working with nested parameter structures.

82.text: RMSNorm as a bare function, no class, no state. JAX style: functions in, functions out.

83.text: Mean of squares, same math as every other lab.

84.text: Divide by the root mean square to normalize. This keeps all vectors at roughly unit scale, preventing activations from growing or shrinking as they flow through layers.

87.text: The forward pass is a PURE FUNCTION: same inputs always produce the same outputs, no side effects. This is what makes jax.grad and jax.jit work, they need mathematical functions, not stateful objects.

93.text: Sequence length from the input shape.

94.text: Token embedding lookup via array indexing, same operation as PyTorch's nn.Embedding.

95.text: Position embeddings tell the model where each token sits in the sequence. Without these, the model would treat 'ab' and 'ba' identically.

96.text: Combine token and position embeddings and normalize before entering the transformer.

98.text: Iterate through each transformer layer. With one layer this runs once, but the code generalizes to deeper stacks.

99.text: Save the current hidden state so we can add it back after attention as a residual connection.

100.text: Normalize before the attention sub-layer. Pre-norm keeps activations stable so the attention mechanism receives well-scaled inputs.

102.text: Matrix multiply for Q projection. Explicit @ with the parameter array, no hidden weight storage.

103.text: Project the normalized input into key vectors that other positions will query against.

104.text: Project the normalized input into value vectors carrying content to aggregate.

107.text: Multi-head reshape, same transpose pattern as PyTorch.

108.text: Reshape keys into separate attention heads, matching the same multi-head pattern as queries.

109.text: Reshape values into separate attention heads for parallel processing.

111.text: Scaled dot-product attention. Same math, different framework.

112.text: Causal mask using JAX's array ops.

113.text: Apply mask with jnp.where, JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's masked_fill.

116.text: Multiply attention weights by value vectors. Tokens deemed relevant by the query-key scores contribute their content proportionally to the output.

117.text: Transpose and reshape heads back to (n, D).

118.text: Output projection + residual in one line.

121.text: Save the hidden state before the MLP for the second residual connection in this block.

122.text: Normalize before the feed-forward network, following the pre-norm transformer pattern.

123.text: Multiply by the expansion weights to widen the representation for richer transformations.

124.text: Apply ReLU activation to introduce nonlinearity between the two MLP linear layers.

125.text: Contract the MLP back to embedding dimension and add the residual. This completes the second sub-layer of the transformer block.

131.text: Loss function, also a pure function. Takes params, inputs, and targets; returns a scalar loss. This is exactly what jax.grad expects.

133.text: Run the forward pass inside the loss function. grad will differentiate through this entire computation.

134.text: log_softmax is numerically more stable than separate softmax + log.

135.text: Get the number of tokens in the input sequence for indexing into log probabilities.

136.text: Gather target log-probs and negate their mean. Cross-entropy in one expression.

141.text: The JAX magic: grad(loss_fn) returns a NEW FUNCTION that computes gradients. jit compiles it. No backward pass code, no gradient tape, just function transformation.

142.text: JIT-compile the loss function too for fast evaluation.

147.text: Same hyperparameters as all previous labs.

148.text: jax.tree.map applies zeros_like to every array in the params dict. Functional alternative to manually creating matching zero buffers.

149.text: Second moment state, same pytree structure.

152.text: Adam update as a pure function, takes old state, returns new state. No mutation. This is the functional paradigm: transform data, don't change it.

153.text: Allocate fresh output dictionaries instead of mutating the old ones. In JAX's functional style, immutability makes transformations like jit and grad safe.

156.text: Iterate over each parameter name to apply the same Adam formula. The dict structure mirrors the model's parameter layout.

157.text: First moment update, same formula as all previous labs.

158.text: Update the exponential moving average of squared gradients. This tracks gradient magnitude per parameter, enabling adaptive learning rates.

159.text: Correct the first moment estimate for its bias toward zero in early training steps.

160.text: Correct the second moment estimate for its initialization bias in early steps.

161.text: Parameter update. Creates a NEW array; the old one is untouched.

162.text: Return the complete new state, params, first moments, second moments.

168.text: Train for 1000 steps, one document per step. Each iteration: forward, loss, grad, Adam update.

169.text: Iterate over training steps, processing one name per step to update model parameters.

170.text: Cycle through the dataset with modular indexing. After seeing all names, we loop back for another pass.

171.text: Convert the name into a sequence of integer token IDs with BOS markers at both ends.

172.text: Clip to the maximum context window. Most names fit easily, but this guard prevents overflow on unusually long entries.

174.text: Convert the Python token list to a JAX array so it can flow through the JIT-compiled forward pass.

175.text: Wrap the target tokens as a JAX array. These are the correct next characters the model should learn to predict.

177.text: Evaluate the cross-entropy loss using the JIT-compiled function. JIT compilation means this runs as optimized machine code, not interpreted Python.

178.text: Compute gradients using the JIT-compiled gradient function. One call, and JAX's autodiff handles everything, same as PyTorch's loss.backward() but functional.

180.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate toward zero over the course of training.

181.text: Functional Adam update. Returns entirely new params, new moments, nothing is mutated. The old state could be garbage collected.

183.text: Print the loss periodically so we can monitor whether the model is learning.

189.text: Temperature 0.5 makes generation more conservative: the model favors high-confidence predictions, producing more typical-sounding names.

191.text: JAX's PRNG needs explicit keys for every random operation during inference too.

192.text: Generate 20 brand-new names by sampling from the model's learned distribution.

193.text: Begin each generated name with the beginning-of-sequence token as the initial prompt.

194.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the maximum sequence length. Each step feeds all generated tokens back through the model.

195.text: Convert growing token list to JAX array.

196.text: Forward pass, the same pure function, just called without gradient tracking.

197.text: Grab the logits for the last generated position and divide by temperature. Lower values make the distribution sharper and more predictable.

198.text: jax.random.split produces two new keys from one, JAX's way of consuming randomness without hidden state. Every random call needs a unique key.

199.text: categorical samples from log-probabilities (unnormalized logits). JAX's equivalent of PyTorch's multinomial.

200.text: When the model outputs the BOS token during generation, it signals the name is complete and we stop adding characters.

202.text: Append the newly sampled token to the growing list so it becomes part of the context for predicting the next character.

203.text: Map each token ID back to its character, skipping the leading BOS, to assemble the generated name as a readable string.

204.text: Print the finished name. These are novel names the model invented by learning the statistical patterns of 32K real names.
